carpet.rule.insaneBehaviors.desc: Makes the random velocities of droppers and projectiles (as well as both the position and velocity of blocks broken by pistons) sampled from a chosen distribution.
carpet.rule.ibDistribution.desc: The type of random distribution to use for IBCarpet.
carpet.rule.ibDiscreteCuts.desc: Controls how many possible evenly spaced values the discrete random number generator may produce on [0,1]. Think of this as evenly chopping up a log and the position of the cuts represent the value of the discrete distribution. When there's 0 cuts, the only possible values are 0 and 1. When there is 1 cut, the possible values are 0, 0.5, and 1.  When there are 3 cuts, the possible values are now 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1. The larger the cuts, the similar it is to the uniform distribution. This distribution superseded the RNG used in JoaCarpet because each random number sampled from the distribution is independent from one another and it doesn't require any manual inputs to reset the RNG when using it for a prolonged period of time. JoaCarpet has an issue with items ending up in a near-similar spot because it's resolution value got too big when using it for a long time.
carpet.rule.ibBimodalExtremeness.desc: Controls how extreme you want the bimodal distribution to be. 0 means uniform distribution. Values close to 1 means larger probability of generating more extreme values.
carpet.rule.ibTrimodalExtremeness.desc: Controls how extreme you want the trimodal distribution to be. 0 means uniform distribution. Values close to 1 means larger probability of generating more extreme values and values closer to 0.
